I would like to talk something through with you, when you have a moment.
Before I say anything, how do you see the last quarter?
I want to be candid with you, and I want this to stay constructive.
I would rather address this now than let it sit and grow.
Let us clear the air and agree on one next step together.
So, you just spoke with Bruno about his numbers. How did it go? I know you were dreading it.
Better than I feared. I almost opened with, your results are down, but I caught myself. Instead I said, I was hoping we could look at the last quarter together. It set a completely different tone.
That is the whole game. The moment people feel attacked, they get defensive and stop hearing you. Did you lead with a question?

I did. I asked what had been getting in his way, and I really listened. Showing a little empathy first meant he did not have to defend himself; he actually owned up to dropping the ball on two accounts.
Good. And were you still candid about the problem? Empathy is not the same as going soft.
I was. I was candid about the gap, but I kept it constructive; we talked about fixes, not blame. I think that is what defused the tension and protected his morale.
Exactly. You separated the person from the problem. That is how you address a hard issue and still clear the air.
Right. Be assertive about the issue, gentle with the person. I think we avoided a lot of fallout by just talking honestly.
I was hoping we could be candid about the gap and keep it constructive.
A little empathy helped defuse it, and one honest talk cleared the air.
I was hoping we could look at the last quarter together.
Before I say anything, how do you see it?
I want to be candid, and I want this to stay constructive.
I noticed something I would like to talk through, not blame you for.

Okay, in ten minutes I have to talk to Bruno about last quarter, and I am not looking forward to it. His numbers dropped, two accounts slipped, and I need to address it honestly. But I have learned something the hard way: if I open with You missed your targets, he will get defensive, shut down, and nothing will change. So let me plan this. I will not lead with the problem; I will lead with a question. I was hoping we could look at the quarter together, and then, what do you think got in the way? Listen first. Show some empathy before I say anything hard. He is a good person having a rough run, not a lazy one. Then I will be candid about the gap, because pretending it is fine helps no one, but I will keep it constructive: we talk about fixes, not blame. My job is to defuse the tension, not to win. If I get this right, he owns up to what happened, we agree on a clear next step, and his morale survives. Be assertive about the issue, gentle with the person. That is the whole skill. Right. Let me go.

Hi Victor, it is Renata from People and Culture. Before your conversation with Bruno, three quick reminders. First, separate the person from the problem. Be candid about the result, but never attack the person; the moment someone feels judged, they stop listening and start defending. So describe what you saw, not who he is. Second, lead with a question, not a verdict. Ask what has been getting in his way and actually listen to the answer. A little empathy early does more to defuse tension than any clever phrase. And use tentative language to soften the opening: I was hoping we could, I was wondering if, it would help if. It lowers the temperature without hiding the message. Third, do not end the talk in the air. Agree on one clear next step together, so he can own up to what happened and walk out with his morale intact. Address the issue, clear the air, and protect the relationship. You are good at this, Victor. Go in calm, stay curious, and you will be fine.
